text: Active recovery at 80% of second ventilatory threshold velocity between high-intensity intervals does not increase time spent above 90% VO2max compared to passive recovery in well-trained runners.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)

text: Active recovery between high-intensity intervals elicits higher mean VO2 across the entire session compared to passive recovery.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)

text: Passive recovery between high-intensity intervals results in lower perceived exertion (RPE) than active recovery, despite similar cardiorespiratory stimulus.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)

text: Active recovery between high-intensity intervals results in lower post-session blood lactate compared to passive recovery.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)

text: Passive recovery is preferable to active recovery during aerobic interval sessions (4×2 min at 100% MAS) because it achieves a similar cardiorespiratory training stimulus with lower perceptual load.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)

text: Countermovement jump performance is potentiated during high-intensity aerobic interval sessions regardless of whether active or passive recovery is used between intervals.
population: well-trained male distance runners (VO2max ~59 mL/kg/min, ~35 min 10K)
